---
title: "Mapping the AdTech Ecosystem with BigQuery Graphs"
description: "Using BigQuery's native Graph Query Language (GQL) to analyze ads.txt and sellers.json relationships across the HTTP Archive web crawl."
date: "2026-04-12"
categories: ["BigQuery", "Data Engineering", "AdTech"]
code-links:
  - text: Open in Colab
    icon: google
    href: https://colab.research.google.com/github/max-ostapenko/website-source/blob/main/src/posts/ads_almanac/notebook.ipynb
---

## Introduction

In modern web advertising, the relationships between publishers, supply-side platforms (SSPs), and demand-side platforms (DSPs) form a sprawling graph of trust and money. A single publisher might use dozens of SSPs, which in turn resell inventory through intermediary chains tracked in `ads.txt` and `sellers.json` files.

Querying this "supply chain" with standard SQL requires fragile `WITH RECURSIVE` CTEs that are hard to read and hard to scale. BigQuery recently introduced native [Property Graphs and Graph Query Language (GQL)](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/graph-intro), letting us model and traverse these multi-hop relationships directly.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Dataset Limitations

This analysis uses the [HTTP Archive](https://httparchive.org/) monthly web crawl. Before drawing conclusions keep the following structural constraints in mind:

- **Coverage bias** ‚Äî Only top-ranked pages are crawled. Long-tail publishers ‚Äî often the most affected by supply chain opacity ‚Äî are absent from the data.
- **Monthly snapshot** ‚Äî `ads.txt` and `sellers.json` files change constantly (additions, revocations, corrections). A single monthly crawl captures a point-in-time view.
- **Google patched via direct download** ‚Äî Google's `sellers.json` (~120 MB) is [skipped by the HTTP Archive crawler](https://support.google.com/authorizedbuyers/answer/9895942) due to a 5-second HTTP fetch timeout. Step 1c in the notebook downloads it directly from `storage.googleapis.com/adx-rtb-dictionaries/sellers.json`, normalises domains with `NET.REG_DOMAIN()` in BigQuery, and appends them to `sellers_json_relationships` before the graph is built. Without this step `google.com` would have no `RepresentsPublisher` edges despite appearing in millions of `ads.txt` DIRECT entries.
- **Confidential sellers hidden** ‚Äî SSPs can mark sellers as `is_confidential = 1`. The crawler counts these entries but drops the domain, so those publisher-platform relationships are invisible.
- **Domain-level only** ‚Äî The crawler deduplicates by domain (a JS `Set`), not by `seller_id`. Cross-checking the exact account record in `ads.txt` against the exact seller in `sellers.json` is therefore not possible ‚Äî matches are domain-level approximations.
- **5-second HTTP timeout** ‚Äî Slow or oversized `sellers.json` files are silently dropped, biasing the dataset toward fast-serving SSPs.

**Better datasets for deeper compliance analysis**: crawl the SSP universe yourself (there are ~500‚Äì1,000 active SSPs so full coverage is achievable); use [Scope3](https://www.scope3.com/) (commercial, supply chain + carbon focus); [Pixalate](https://pixalate.com/) (commercial, IVT + compliance); or the IAB Tech Lab's [ads.txt compliance tools](https://iabtechlab.com/ads-txt/).
:::

## The Advertising Supply Chain

To understand the graph schema it helps to know who the actors are and which protocols they use to declare their relationships.

### Participants

| Term | Role | Graph element |
|---|---|---|
| **Publisher** | Website or app with ad inventory to sell (e.g. nytimes.com) | `Publisher` node |
| **SSP** ‚Äî Supply-Side Platform | Helps publishers sell inventory programmatically; runs the auction (e.g. Pubmatic, OpenX, Rubicon) | `AdTechPlatform` node |
| **DSP** ‚Äî Demand-Side Platform | Buys inventory on behalf of advertisers (e.g. DV360, The Trade Desk) | `AdTechPlatform` node |
| **Ad Exchange** | Marketplace connecting SSPs and DSPs; often operated by an SSP itself | `AdTechPlatform` node |

### Declaration Protocols

**`ads.txt`** is a plain-text file that publishers host at `publisher.com/ads.txt`. Each line authorises one seller:

```
pubmatic.com,  123456,    DIRECT,   f5ab79cb980f11d1
openx.com,     537153765, RESELLER
```

- `DIRECT` ‚Äî highest trust. The publisher explicitly authorises this platform to sell their inventory without an intermediary.
- `RESELLER` ‚Äî lower trust. The publisher allows the platform to resell through a third party. Legitimacy is verified by cross-referencing the platform's own `sellers.json`.

**`sellers.json`** is the SSP-side counterpart hosted at `ssp.com/sellers.json`. It declares every entity the SSP represents or routes through:

| `seller_type` | Meaning | Graph edge |
|---|---|---|
| `publisher` | SSP directly represents this domain's inventory | `RepresentsPublisher` (Platform ‚Üí Publisher) |
| `intermediary` | SSP sells through another SSP ‚Äî a hop in the supply chain | `SellsThrough` (Platform ‚Üí Platform) |
| `both` | Partner is simultaneously publisher AND intermediary | Both edge types |

::: {.callout-note}
The HTTP Archive crawler stores these files under the `//[ads]` custom metric key. Inside `custom_metrics.other` the JSON paths are: `$.ads.ads.*` for `ads.txt` and `$.ads.sellers.*` for `sellers.json`.
:::

### Graph Schema

```
Publisher   ‚Üê‚îÄ[DirectBid]‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ AdTechPlatform
Publisher   ‚Üê‚îÄ[ResellerBid]‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ AdTechPlatform
Publisher   ‚Üê‚îÄ[RepresentsPublisher]‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ AdTechPlatform
AdTechPlatform ‚Üê‚îÄ[SellsThrough]‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ AdTechPlatform
```

The `SellsThrough` edges form the intermediary graph that the original `WITH RECURSIVE` CTE was tracing step by step. GQL's `{1,3}` path quantifier replaces the entire recursion ‚Äî and prevents revisiting nodes without any extra cycle-guard logic.

## Building and Querying the Graph

The notebook below builds the full pipeline: from raw HTTP Archive data to a queryable BigQuery property graph, then runs four analysis queries in GQL.

{{< embed ./notebook.ipynb echo=true >}}
